content:
4 min read
Just now

--

For a long time, I thought bugs were proof that I wasn’t a good programmer.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Bernd 📷 Dittrich on UnsplashEvery time my code crashed, I felt like I’d done something wrong.
So I’d immediately start fixing it without asking an important question:
Why did it break in the first place?
Looking back, I realize my biggest improvements as a Python developer didn’t come from courses, books, or tutorials.
They came from breaking my own code.
Over and over again.
Every frustrating error message, unexpected exception, and mysterious bug taught me something that no tutorial ever could.
These are the seven lessons that completely changed the way I write Python.
1. Code That Works Isn’t Always Good Code
One of my earliest projects finally ran without errors.
I celebrated.
A week later, I opened the file to add one small feature.
It took me nearly an hour just to understand my own code.
That’s when I realized something.
Working code isn’t the finish line.
Readable code is.
Today, I optimize for clarity before cleverness.
Future me is one of the most important people who will read my code.
2. Small Functions Prevent Big Problems
I used to write enormous functions that handled everything.
Reading files.
Cleaning data.
Processing results.
Saving output.
Printing reports.
When something broke, finding the bug felt like searching for a needle in a haystack.
Now I keep functions focused on one responsibility.
They’re easier to test.
Easier to reuse.
And much easier to debug.
Breaking one small function is far less painful than breaking one giant one.
3. Error Messages Are Trying to Help You
For months, I treated Python’s exceptions like enemies.
I ignored stack traces.
I searched Google for the last line of the error.
I copied fixes without understanding them.
Eventually, I started reading error messages carefully.
Most of them were already telling me exactly where the problem started.
Now the traceback is usually the first thing I investigate — not the last.
4. Never Trust Input
One of my scripts worked perfectly…
Until someone entered unexpected data.
An empty string.
A missing value.
A filename that didn’t exist.
Everything failed.
That experience taught me an important lesson.
Programs rarely fail because users follow instructions.
They fail because real users don’t.
Now I validate inputs before assuming anything.
It saves countless debugging sessions later.
5. Repeating Yourself Is a Warning Sign
Whenever I find myself copying the same code three or four times, I stop.
There’s almost always a better solution.
A function.
A class.
A loop.
A reusable helper.
Duplicated code feels convenient today.
It becomes technical debt tomorrow.
6. Debugging Is a Skill, Not Bad Luck
I used to think experienced developers rarely debugged code.
Then I worked with better programmers.
They debug constantly.
The difference is how they approach it.
They form hypotheses.
Test one thing at a time.
Read logs.
Inspect variables.
Eliminate possibilities systematically.
They don’t guess.
They investigate.
Learning to debug well improved my programming far more than memorizing syntax ever did.
7. Every Bug Teaches Something
The bugs I remember most weren’t the hardest ones.
They were the ones who exposed bad assumptions.
Assuming a list wasn’t empty.
Assuming a file always existed.
Assuming an API would always return the same response.
Assuming users would behave exactly as I expected.
Each bug challenged one of those assumptions.
Over time, I stopped asking,
“Why did Python break?”
Instead, I started asking,
“What assumption did I make that wasn’t true?”
That small change completely transformed the way I solve problems.
The Lesson I Wish I’d Learned Earlier
Programming isn’t about avoiding mistakes.
It’s about learning from them faster.
Every developer, no matter how experienced , writes bugs.
The difference is that experienced developers use those bugs to improve how they think.
Every broken program teaches something about design.
Every confusing traceback teaches something about debugging.
Every failed project teaches something about writing better software.
Those lessons compound over time.
Final Thoughts
Some of my most valuable Python lessons didn’t come from perfectly written code.
They came from code that failed spectacularly.
Projects that crashed.
Functions that returned nonsense.
Scripts that worked yesterday but somehow broke today.
At the time, those moments felt frustrating.
Now I’m grateful for them.
Because every bug forced me to become a slightly better programmer.
Today, I still break my own code.
Just not for the same reasons.
And every time it happens, I know there’s probably another lesson waiting on the other side.
